# Standard Error — The Universal Reference

## Learning Goals

By the end of this lesson, students should be able to:

1. Explain in plain English what standard error measures
2. Identify which SE formula applies in each inferential context
3. Recognize that CI and test statistic formulas share the same SE structure
4. Compute SE for means, proportions, and regression coefficients

## In Plain English

Standard error (SE) is the **standard deviation of a sampling distribution** — it measures how uncertain your estimate is. Every confidence interval and test statistic divides by one.

The structure is always the same regardless of what you're estimating:

$$\text{CI}: \quad \hat{\theta} \pm (\text{critical value}) \times SE(\hat{\theta})$$

$$\text{Test statistic}: \quad \frac{\hat{\theta} - \theta_0}{SE(\hat{\theta})}$$

Larger SE → wider CI → harder to detect a difference.

## SE Master Table

### Means

| Situation | SE Formula | Distribution |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| One-sample, σ known | $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$ | Z |
| One-sample, σ unknown | $s / \sqrt{n}$ | t, df = n−1 |
| Two-sample pooled | $s_p\sqrt{1/n_1 + 1/n_2}$ | t, df = n₁+n₂−2 |
| Two-sample Welch | $\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2}$ | t, df = ν (Welch) |
| Paired | $s_d / \sqrt{n}$ | t, df = n−1 |

$$s_p = \sqrt{\frac{(n_1-1)s_1^2 + (n_2-1)s_2^2}{n_1+n_2-2}}$$

### Proportions

| Situation | SE Formula |
|-----------|-----------|
| Hypothesis test (use null p₀) | $\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}$ |
| Confidence interval (use p̂) | $\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}$ |

### Regression

| Parameter | SE Formula |
|-----------|-----------|
| SLR slope β̂₁ | $\sqrt{MSE / S_{xx}}$ |
| MLR coefficient β̂ⱼ | $\sqrt{MSE \cdot C_{jj}}$ |
| CI on mean response | $\sqrt{MSE[1/n + (x_0-\bar{x})^2/S_{xx}]}$ |
| PI on future obs | adds +1 under the square root |

> R gives `se(β̂ⱼ)` in the `Std. Error` column of `summary(fit)`.

## R Example


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Simulate a sample and compute SE
set.seed(42)
x <- rnorm(30, mean = 50, sd = 10)

n    <- length(x)
xbar <- mean(x)
s    <- sd(x)
se   <- s / sqrt(n)

cat("n =", n, "\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
n = 30 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
cat("x̄ =", round(xbar, 3), "\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
x̄ = 50.686 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
cat("s =", round(s, 3), "\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
s = 12.55 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
cat("SE = s/√n =", round(se, 3), "\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
SE = s/√n = 2.291 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
cat("95% CI: (", round(xbar - 1.96*se, 2), ",", round(xbar + 1.96*se, 2), ")\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
95% CI: ( 46.19 , 55.18 )
```


:::
:::


## Why the SE Changes Shape

All SEs follow the same logic:

1. Start with the **variance of the estimate** (how variable is θ̂?)
2. Take the **square root** → standard deviation of the estimate = SE
3. **n appears in the denominator** → more data = smaller SE = narrower CI

For regression, MSE plays the role of σ² (variance estimated from residuals), and the extra terms account for the position of x₀ relative to the data centroid.

## Try It Yourself

Modify the code above:

1. Change `n` from 30 to 100. How does SE change?
2. Change `sd` from 10 to 20. How does SE change?
3. What happens to the 95% CI as n → ∞?
